perturbation.original_text: As used herein, the term “Proprietary Information” refers to any and all Information of a confidential, proprietary, or secret nature which is applicable to or related In any way to (i) the business, present or future, of the Disclosing Party, (ii) the research and development or investigations of the Disclosing Party or (iii) the business of any customer of the Disclosing Party; provided , in each case , that such information is delivered to the Receiving Party by the Disclosing Party and (a) is marked or identified in writing as “Confidential”, (b) if verbal or visual disclosure, is identified as “Confidential” in a writing within ten (10) business days of such disclosure, or (c) is information that the Receiving Party actually knew or reasonably should have known was confidential.
perturbation.changed_text: As used herein, the term “Proprietary Information” refers to any and all Information of a confidential, proprietary, or secret nature which is applicable to or related In any way to (i) the business, present or future, of the Disclosing Party, (ii) the research and development or investigations of the Disclosing Party or (iii) the business of any customer of the Disclosing Party; provided , in each case , that such information is delivered to the Receiving Party by the Disclosing Party and (a) is marked or identified in writing as “Confidential”, (b) if verbal or visual disclosure, is identified as “Confidential” in a writing within thirty (30) business days of such disclosure, or (c) is information that the Receiving Party actually knew or reasonably should have known was confidential.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an in-text contradiction. The original text states that verbal or visual disclosures identified as confidential must be confirmed in writing within ten (10) business days. Changing this to thirty (30) business days creates a direct conflict, leading to uncertainty about the actual timeframe for classifying verbal or visual disclosures as Proprietary Information. This is further contradicted when the text later references adhering to the 'terms set forth herein,' with it being unclear which timeline to follow.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Receiving Party agrees (a) to hold the Disclosing Party’s Proprietary Information In strict confidence and to take all reasonable precautions to protect such Proprietary Information (including, without limitation, all precautions that Receiving Party employs with respect to its confidential materials), (b) not to divulge any such Proprietary Information or any information derived therefrom to any third person without prior written permission of the Disclosing Party, excluding disclosure to Representatives, (c) not to make any use whatsoever at any time of such Proprietary Information except to evaluate internally whether to enter into the contemplated business relationship with the Disclosing Party, and (d) not to copy or reverse engineer any such Proprietary Information. Any employee given access to any such Proprietary Information must have a legitimate “need to know” and be subject to restrictions on disclosure of Proprietary Information practically identical to the terms set forth herein. Without granting any right or license, the Disclosing Party agrees that the foregoing clauses (a), (b) and (c) shall not apply with respect to any information that (i) is or (through no improper action or inaction by the Receiving Party or any agent, consultant or employee) becomes generally available to the public, or (ii) was or is rightfully in Receiving Party’s possession or the possession of any of its Representatives or was known by it or its Representatives without restriction prior to receipt from the Disclosing Party, or (iii) was or is rightfully disclosed to Receiving Party or any of its Representatives by a third party provided the Receiving Party complies with restrictions imposed by the third party, or (iv) was or is independently developed without use of any Proprietary Information of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the timeframe for identifying verbal or visual disclosures as confidential from ten (10) to thirty (30) business days. The contradicted text states that any employee given access to any such Proprietary Information must have a legitimate “need to know” and be subject to restrictions on disclosure of Proprietary Information practically identical to the terms set forth herein. The contradiction arises because it's unclear which timeframe (10 or 30 days) the employee should adhere to. The original text, as it existed, creates consistency, where all parties agree to the same standards and terms. This change creates an in-text contradiction because the receiving party is held to 'terms set forth herein', but there are now two different terms/timelines within the same contract.

perturbation.original_text: Promptly upon receipt of a written request by the Disclosing Party at any time (which will be effective if actually received or three days after mailed first class postage prepaid to the Receiving Party’s address herein), the Receiving Party will turn over to the Disclosing Party, or, at its sole discretion, destroy and certify to the Receiving party in writing the destruction of all Proprietary Information of the Disclosing Party and all documents or media containing any such Proprietary Information and any and all copies or extracts thereof; provided, however, the Company and its Representatives shall not be obligated to return or destroy Proprietary Information to the extent otherwise required by law, regulation, rule or practice governing professionals or any internal compliance policy or procedure required by law, regulation rule of practice governing professionals.
perturbation.changed_text: Promptly upon receipt of a written request by the Disclosing Party at any time (which will be effective if actually received or five days after mailed first class postage prepaid to the Receiving Party’s address herein), the Receiving Party will turn over to the Disclosing Party, or, at its sole discretion, retain for archival purposes all Proprietary Information of the Disclosing Party and all documents or media containing any such Proprietary Information and any and all copies or extracts thereof; provided, however, the Company and its Representatives shall not be obligated to retain Proprietary Information to the extent otherwise required by law, regulation, rule or practice governing professionals or any internal compliance policy or procedure required by law, regulation rule of practice governing professionals.
perturbation.explanation: This change creates an in-text contradiction by modifying both the timeframe for the effectiveness of a return request and the Receiving Party's obligation upon such request. Originally, the request was effective after three days, and the Receiving Party was required to return or destroy the proprietary information. Now, the request is effective after five days, and the Receiving Party is obligated to retain, not destroy, the information for archival purposes. This directly contradicts the original intent of immediate return or destruction, leading to uncertainty about the Receiving Party's obligations. Later sections, such as Section 5, assume a potential for breach of the agreement by unauthorized release, which directly contrasts with the newly mandated archival retention of information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The parties acknowledge and agree that due to the unique nature of the possible transaction between the parties, there can be no adequate remedy at law for any material breach of this Agreement, that any such breach may allow the breaching party or third parties to unfairly compete with the non-breaching party, resulting in irreparable harm to the non-breaching party, and therefore, that upon any such breach or any threat thereof, the non-breaching party shall be entitled to seek appropriate equitable relief in addition to whatever remedies it might have at law. The Receiving Party will notify the Disclosing Party In writing immediately upon the occurrence of any unauthorized release or other breach of which it is aware.
perturbation.justification: The changed text mandates retention of proprietary information for archival purposes, directly contradicting the original requirement to return or destroy such information upon request. This creates a conflict with the contradicted text, which addresses the unauthorized release or breach of the agreement. The requirement to retain information for archival purposes increases the risk of unauthorized release, creating a direct in-text contradiction. The original text was centered around ensuring information was not released or used after the agreement ended, whereas the changed text creates a conflicting scenario where the Receiving Party must retain information.

perturbation.original_text: During the one year period commencing on the date of this Agreement, the Company will not solicit or seek to employ any key employee or executive officer of Actuate or any subsidiary or other affiliate of Actuate with whom the Company had contact or became aware of in connection with the possible transaction; provided, however, that the foregoing provisions shall not prohibit the solicitation or employment of any such person (i) resulting from general advertisements for employment conducted by the Company (including any recruitment efforts conducted by any recruitment agency, provided that the Company has not directed such recruitment efforts at such person), (ii) if such person approaches the Company on an unsolicited basis or (iii) following the cessation of such person’s employment with Actuate without any solicitation or encouragement by the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: During the one year period commencing on the date of this Agreement, the Company will not solicit or seek to employ any key employee or executive officer of Actuate or any subsidiary or other affiliate of Actuate with whom the Company had contact or became aware of in connection with the possible transaction; provided, however, that the foregoing provisions shall not prohibit the solicitation or employment of any such person (i) resulting from general advertisements for employment conducted by the Company (including any recruitment efforts conducted by any recruitment agency, provided that the Company has not directed such recruitment efforts at such person), (ii) if such person approaches the Company on an unsolicited basis or (iii) following the cessation of such person’s employment with Actuate with or without solicitation or encouragement by the Company.
perturbation.explanation: This perturbation introduces an in-text contradiction by removing the condition that cessation of employment must be *without* solicitation or encouragement from the Company for the exception to apply. Now, the Company can hire former Actuate employees even if they encouraged the employee to leave Actuate, as long as the person is no longer employed by Actuate. This directly conflicts with the overall intent of the clause, which is to prevent the Company from actively poaching Actuate's employees. This also contradicts Section 6, by allowing the company to poach employees that are similar to, or competitive with, products, services, and other materials.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The parties expressly agree that neither the terms or conditions of this Agreement, nor the discussions held by the parties to address the feasibility of a potential business relationship shall be construed as to prevent either party from pursuing similar discussions with third parties in similar markets or obligate either party to take, continue or forgo any action relating to the above-mentioned discussions. Further, nothing in this Agreement shall be construed as prohibiting or restricting either party from independently developing, acquiring, and marketing products, services, and other materials, which are similar to or competitive In any geographic area and in any form with the other party’s product(s) or service(s). However, this paragraph shall not be deemed to grant to either party any license under the other party’s copyrights or patents.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the requirement that the cessation of employment with Actuate must be without solicitation or encouragement by the Company for the exception to the non-solicitation clause to apply. This allows the Company to potentially 'poach' employees from Actuate, as long as they are no longer employed, even if the company encouraged their departure. This contradicts the original intent of the clause, which aims to prevent active solicitation of Actuate employees. It also contradicts the section stating that the agreement should not prevent either party from independently developing, acquiring, and marketing products, services, and other materials, which are similar to or competitive with the other party’s product(s) or service(s). The changed text enables the company to specifically target and solicit talent from Actuate to enhance their competitive positioning, hence creating a contradiction. Therefore, according to the perturbation type 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction', there is a strong contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: From the date of this Agreement until May 31 , 2015 (the “Standstill Period”) the Company will not, in any manner, directly or indirectly through any third party: (a) make, effect, initiate, cause or participate in (i) any acquisition of beneficial ownership of any securities of Actuate or any securities of any subsidiary or other affiliate of Actuate, (ii) any acquisition of any assets of Actuate or any assets of any subsidiary or other affiliate of Actuate, (iii) any tender offer, exchange offer, merger. business combination, recapitalization, restructuring, liquidation, dissolution or extraordinary transaction involving Actuate or any subsidiary or other affiliate of Actuate, or involving any securities or assets of Actuate or any securities or assets of any subsidiary or other affiliate of Actuate, or (iv) any “solicitation” of “proxies” (as those terms are used in the proxy rules of the Securities and Exchange Commission) or consents with respect to any securities of Actuate; (b) form, join or participate in a “group” (as defined in the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 and the rules promulgated thereunder) with respect to the beneficial ownership of any securities of Actuate; (c) act, alone or in concert with others, to seek to control or influence the management, board of directors or policies of Actuate; (d) take any action that might require Actuate to make a public announcement regarding any of the types of matters set forth in clause “(a)” of this sentence; (e) agree or offer to take, or encourage or propose (publicly or otherwise) the taking of, any action referred to in clause “(a) “, “(b)”, “(c)” or “(d)” of this sentence; or (f) assist, induce or encourage any other individual and any corporation, partnership, entity, group, tribunal or governmental authority (“Person”) to take any action of the type referred to In clause “(a)”, “(b)”, “(c)”, “(d)” or “(e)” of this sentence. (g) enter into any discussions, negotiations, arrangement or agreement with any other Person relating to any of the foregoing; or Notwithstanding the foregoing, the Company is permitted to purchase any securities that does not in any event result in an aggregate ownership by the Company of more than 5% of the outstanding amount of any class of securities of Actuate or of any subsidiary or other affiliate of Actuate.
perturbation.changed_text: From the date of this Agreement until May 31 , 2014 (the “Standstill Period”) the Company will not, in any manner, directly or indirectly through any third party: (a) make, effect, initiate, cause or participate in (i) any acquisition of beneficial ownership of any securities of Actuate or any securities of any subsidiary or other affiliate of Actuate, (ii) any acquisition of any assets of Actuate or any assets of any subsidiary or other affiliate of Actuate, (iii) any tender offer, exchange offer, merger. business combination, recapitalization, restructuring, liquidation, dissolution or extraordinary transaction involving Actuate or any subsidiary or other affiliate of Actuate, or involving any securities or assets of Actuate or any securities or assets of any subsidiary or other affiliate of Actuate, or (iv) any “solicitation” of “proxies” (as those terms are used in the proxy rules of the Securities and Exchange Commission) or consents with respect to any securities of Actuate; (b) form, join or participate in a “group” (as defined in the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 and the rules promulgated thereunder) with respect to the beneficial ownership of any securities of Actuate; (c) act, alone or in concert with others, to seek to control or influence the management, board of directors or policies of Actuate; (d) take any action that might require Actuate to make a public announcement regarding any of the types of matters set forth in clause “(a)” of this sentence; (e) agree or offer to take, or encourage or propose (publicly or otherwise) the taking of, any action referred to in clause “(a) “, “(b)”, “(c)” or “(d)” of this sentence; or (f) assist, induce or encourage any other individual and any corporation, partnership, entity, group, tribunal or governmental authority (“Person”) to take any action of the type referred to In clause “(a)”, “(b)”, “(c)”, “(d)” or “(e)” of this sentence. (g) enter into any discussions, negotiations, arrangement or agreement with any other Person relating to any of the foregoing; or Notwithstanding the foregoing, the Company is permitted to purchase any securities that does not in any event result in an aggregate ownership by the Company of more than 5% of the outstanding amount of any class of securities of Actuate or of any subsidiary or other affiliate of Actuate.
perturbation.explanation: The Standstill Period originally ended on May 31, 2015. Changing the end date to May 31, 2014 creates an in-text contradiction with Section 9 which states 'This Agreement shall expire and cease to have any force or effect on the earlier of (i) eighteen months from the date hereof or (ii) the date of consummation of a transaction between the parties.' Since the agreement was signed in July 24, 2014, 18 months from this date is January 24, 2016. As such, making the Standstill period end May 31, 2014 contradicts Section 9 as this end date has already passed.
perturbation.contradicted_text: This Agreement shall expire and cease to have any force or effect on the earlier of (i) eighteen months from the date hereof or (ii) the date of consummation of a transaction between the parties.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the end date of the Standstill Period from May 31, 2015, to May 31, 2014. The contradicted text states that the agreement expires on the earlier of eighteen months from the date of the agreement (July 24, 2014, meaning January 24, 2016) or the date of a transaction between the parties. The change causes a contradiction because the modified Standstill Period ends on May 31, 2014, a date that has already passed when the agreement was signed, which is incompatible with the clause stating the agreement expires at a later date. This constitutes a strong inconsistency and creates an in-text contradiction as defined.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the law of the State of California without regard to the conflicts of law provisions thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the law of the State of Delaware without regard to the conflicts of law provisions thereof.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an in-text contradiction with Section 10. While it indicates which state law governs the agreement, Actuate Corporation is identified as a Delaware corporation, suggesting its internal affairs are governed by Delaware law, and the agreement makes no explicit mention of needing to follow Delaware law. Switching the choice of law from California to Delaware creates uncertainty and a possible contradiction. This contradiction becomes more apparent because Actuate's principal place of business is in California, which would normally suggest that California law would be most relevant to an agreement like this. However, making the change to Delaware introduces conflict, and therefore, in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: This Mutual Non-Disclosure Agreement (the “Agreement”) is made by and between Actuate Corporation, a Delaware corporation with its principal place of business located at 951 Mariners Island Boulevard, San Mateo, CA 94404 (“Actuate”), and Open Text Corporation, a corporation incorporated pursuant to the laws of Canada, with its principal place of business located at 275 Frank Tompa Drive, Waterloo, ON N2L OA1 (“Company”). Actuate and Company intend to discuss a potential business relationship. Each undersigned party (the “Receiving Party”) and their respective directors, officers, employees, agents, or advisors (including, without limitation, legal advisors, accountants, consultants, bankers, and financial advisors) and potential sources of financing (collectively, “Representatives”) understands that during these discussions the other party (the “Disclosing Party’’) may disclose Proprietary Information (as defined herein) relating to the Disclosing Party’s business. In consideration of the parties’ discussions and any access the Receiving Party may have to Proprietary Information of the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party hereby agrees as follows: 1. As used herein, the term “Proprietary Information” refers to any and all Information of a confidential, proprietary, or secret nature which is applicable to or related In any way to (i) the business, present or future, of the Disclosing Party, (ii) the research and development or investigations of the Disclosing Party or (iii) the business of any customer of the Disclosing Party; provided , in each case , that such information is delivered to the Receiving Party by the Disclosing Party and (a) is marked or identified in writing as “Confidential”, (b) if verbal or visual disclosure, is identified as “Confidential” in a writing within ten (10) business days of such disclosure, or (c) is information that the Receiving Party actually knew or reasonably should have known was confidential. Proprietary Information may include, for example and without limitation, the product offering, content partners, product pricing, product availability, computer programs, technical drawings, algorithms, processes, ideas, schematics, trade secrets, processes, formulas, data, know-how, improvements, inventions (whether patentable or not), techniques, marketing plans, forecasts and strategies, and information concerning customers or vendors. Proprietary Information shall also include all information of a like nature owned by any other person and furnished to the Disclosing Party by such other person pursuant to an undertaking by the Disclosing Party to maintain the same in confidence. 2. The Receiving Party agrees (a) to hold the Disclosing Party’s Proprietary Information In strict confidence and to take all reasonable precautions to protect such Proprietary Information (including, without limitation, all precautions that Receiving Party employs with respect to its confidential materials), (b) not to divulge any such Proprietary Information or any information derived therefrom to any third person without prior written permission of the Disclosing Party, excluding disclosure to Representatives, (c) not to make any use whatsoever at any time of such Proprietary Information except to evaluate internally whether to enter into the contemplated business relationship with the Disclosing Party, and (d) not to copy or reverse engineer any such Proprietary Information. Any employee given access to any such Proprietary Information must have a legitimate “need to know” and be subject to restrictions on disclosure of Proprietary Information practically identical to the terms set forth herein. Without granting any right or license, the Disclosing Party agrees that the foregoing clauses (a), (b) and (c) shall not apply with respect to any information that (i) is or (through no improper action or inaction by the Receiving Party or any agent, consultant or employee) becomes generally available to the public, or (ii) was or is rightfully in Receiving Party’s possession or the possession of any of its Representatives or was known by it or its Representatives without restriction prior to receipt from the Disclosing Party, or (iii) was or is rightfully disclosed to Receiving Party or any of its Representatives by a third party provided the Receiving Party complies with restrictions imposed by the third party, or (iv) was or is independently developed without use of any Proprietary Information of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.justification: The change from California to Delaware law creates an in-text contradiction because the contradicted text explicitly states that Actuate Corporation is a Delaware corporation with its principal place of business in California. This suggests that while Actuate is incorporated in Delaware, its operations and related agreements might be more closely tied to California law. By changing the governing law to Delaware, it introduces a conflict regarding which state's laws primarily govern the agreement, creating uncertainty and a potential legal issue given Actuate's principal place of business. This directly contradicts the implicit understanding and creates uncertainty, meeting the 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement supersedes all prior discussions and writings and constitutes the entire agreement between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof. The prevailing party in any action to enforce this Agreement shall be entitled to reasonable costs and attorneys’ fees.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement supersedes all prior discussions and writings except for any prior agreement regarding trade secrets, and constitutes the agreement between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof. The prevailing party in any action to enforce this Agreement shall not be entitled to reasonable costs and attorneys’ fees.
perturbation.explanation: The change contradicts the "entire agreement" clause by stating it does *not* supersede prior agreements on trade secrets. This creates an in-text contradiction because the original text states that nothing outside of this current agreement matters, while the new text states that other agreements may matter. Additionally, making attorney's fees non-recoverable is a contradiction that adds further financial disincentive to enforcing the agreement and complicates any breach claims referenced in section 5.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The parties acknowledge and agree that due to the unique nature of the possible transaction between the parties, there can be no adequate remedy at law for any material breach of this Agreement, that any such breach may allow the breaching party or third parties to unfairly compete with the non-breaching party, resulting in irreparable harm to the non-breaching party, and therefore, that upon any such breach or any threat thereof, the non-breaching party shall be entitled to seek appropriate equitable relief in addition to whatever remedies it might have at law. The Receiving Party will notify the Disclosing Party In writing immediately upon the occurrence of any unauthorized release or other breach of which it is aware.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a contradiction. The original text states that the agreement supersedes all prior agreements. The changed text states that the agreement supersedes all prior agreements *except* for those regarding trade secrets, thereby creating a direct in-text contradiction. Furthermore, the original text allows for the prevailing party to recover reasonable costs and attorneys’ fees, while the changed text states that they cannot recover such fees. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which presumes that a material breach would allow a non-breaching party to seek appropriate equitable relief in addition to whatever remedies it might have at law.
